metadata.title: RtHon. the Baroness Stowell of Beeston Chair, Communications and Digital Committee House of Lords Lo
metadata.author: nspcc

chunks.text: RtHon. the Baroness Stowell of Beeston Chair, Communications and Digital Committee House of Lords London SW1A 0PW 21 December 2022 DearLady Stowell, I am writing to you following my appearance at the Communications and Digital Committee on the 6 th December. This letter answers the two questions which the Committee asked for me to respond to in writing. Data for coroners and bereaved parents In the evidence session, I spoke of the need for an amendment to the Online Safety Billtohelp bereaved families gain access to the data of a deceased child, where there is a reasonable suspicion that the in for mation is relevant to their death. Currently, bereaved parents are battling with global tech companies, unsuccessfully, to access in for mation that would help them understand the circumstances in which their child died. The Committee asked for my views as to who should be able to receive the data. The NSPCC supports the Bereaved Families for Online Safety Initiative which seeks an amendment to ensure that bereaved parents have a point of contact with in Ofcom, and Ofcom have duties to cooperate with coroners and obtain relevant in for mation on behalf of parents or coroners, where there is reason to suspect that a regulated service provider was involved in the death of a child. This will ensure that families and coroners trying to uncover the cause of the death of a child are not left to the mercy of social media companies and their internal processes. Access to data for researchers and civil society After outlining the case for improving access to data for researchers and civil society, the Committee asked for the NSPCC’s view on the IPO’snewdata mining rules. Un for tunately, the IPO’s rules are not something which the NSPCC has a strong or expert view on and, if possible, I would reframe from commenting. Instead, I would like to clarify that the NSPCC is a rguing for mechanisms to be brought in to the scope of the Bill so that technology companies provide access to data for researchers and civil society. As Frances Haugen, ex-Facebook (now Meta) whistle-blower points out, researchers fromallover the world have spent years warning of the harms caused by social media platforms, whether the damage being to children or the negative impacts of propaganda on democracy. She has called upon law makers to ensure that “organisations with a record of integrity and excellence in research gain access to platform data”. With access to data, civil society will have the tools to hold technology companies to account. I will be happy to provide further in for mation if that is of assistance to the Committee. Yours sincerely, Richard Collard Policy and Regulatory Manager
